serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of corn starch is approximately 1 tablespoon (8 g), used as a thickening agent in sauces, soups, gravies, and desserts.

nutrient_source.title: Corn starch — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for corn starch (cr_id F05040006). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 169715 (Cornstarch) for consistency; HPB values are used as primary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Corn starch GI is reported as approximately 85 (high) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (85 × 7.3) / 100 = 6.2, where carbohydrate per 8 g serving = 91.27 × 0.08 = 7.3 g. The input record cited GI 52 (low) from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved source and contradicts peer-reviewed evidence; the value has been corrected to GI 85 (high) per University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Refined high-GI starch with negligible potassium (3 mg/100g) and minimal fibre (0.9 g/100g); large quantities drive insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Corn starch is low in sodium (9 mg/100g) and is not a direct hypertension risk in typical culinary quantities (5–15 g per dish), but its very high glycaemic load when consumed in excess promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and should be restricted to use as a minor thickening agent only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Pure refined starch with GI ~85 and carbohydrate 91.3 g/100g; causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation even in small quantities
type2_diabetes.guidance: Corn starch has a high glycaemic index (~85) and extremely high carbohydrate density (91.3 g/100g) with negligible fibre (0.9 g/100g), making it unsuitable as a dietary staple for people with Type 2 Diabetes; use must be strictly limited to small thickening quantities (5–10 g per dish) and lower-GI alternatives such as arrowroot or psyllium husk should be considered.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycaemic load; negligible saturated fat but promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia and triglyceride elevation at higher intakes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Corn starch is virtually fat-free (0.05 g/100g) and cholesterol-free, posing minimal direct lipid risk, but its high glycaemic load at culinary-scale use can elevate postprandial triglycerides and contribute to insulin resistance, which are established cardiovascular risk factors; restrict to small thickening quantities and avoid as a primary carbohydrate source.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Corn starch (also spelled cornstarch, or called cornflour in some regions) is a fine, white, odourless powder derived from the endosperm of maize (corn) kernels through a wet-milling and drying process. It is one of the most widely used culinary thickening agents globally, employed in sauces, gravies, soups, puddings, custards, and stir-fry dishes. When mixed with cold water and heated, corn starch gelatinises to form a clear, glossy, semi-transparent gel, distinguishing it from wheat flour-based thickeners which produce an opaque result. Its texture is extremely fine and silky, and it is flavour-neutral, making it suitable for both savoury and sweet applications. Nutritionally, corn starch is almost entirely refined carbohydrate (91.3 g/100g) with negligible protein (0.26 g/100g), minimal fat (0.05 g/100g), and very low fibre (0.9 g/100g). It has a high glycaemic index (~85) and delivers a high glycaemic load even in moderate quantities, making it clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or metabolic syndrome. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. Typical culinary use is 5–15 g per dish as a thickener; it should not be consumed as a primary carbohydrate source. Corn starch is a staple ingredient in Singaporean, Chinese, and Southeast Asian cooking, commonly used to coat meats before stir-frying (velveting technique) and to thicken sauces in dishes such as sweet-and-sour pork, egg drop soup, and various dessert puddings.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05040006, confidence 0.95), cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 169715 for corn starch. GI value corrected from unsupported input value (52, low) to peer-reviewed University of Sydney GI Database value (~85, high). All values are consistent with established food composition literature for refined corn starch.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — corn starch is a pure refined starch with no free sugars present. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB/USDA sources — total fat is only 0.05 g/100g with no measurable saturated fraction. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — corn starch is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1 — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo database for corn starch; total fat is 0.05 g/100g making these fractions nutritionally negligible. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1 — these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for corn starch; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (169715) reports water at approximately 8.3 g/100g but HPB is the primary source for this record and does not report moisture; follow-up with USDA cross-reference recommended for water_g. 3. GI CORRECTION: The input record cited GI 52 (low) from 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved source under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. The GI has been corrected to ~85 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). This correction also invalidates the input health_suitability notes that referenced 'very high GI (~85–95)' while simultaneously citing GI 52 — the corrected value aligns with the clinical narrative. 4. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Corn starch is a generic global ingredient, not a cuisine-specific dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. No Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification applies. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code cited are defensible matches from published ontology resources; FoodEx2 code A00LC (Maize starch) is the standard FoodEx2 entry for this ingredient.
